rulings.Case Title: VANESSA KING et al VS. AMY QU LUCIER et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside The Dismissal Of Defendant Amy Qu Lucier
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 13, PLAINTIFFS VANESSA KING AND AMARITA KING'S Motion To Set Aside The Dismissal Of Defendant Amy Qu Lucier Plaintiff's motion to set aside dismissal of Defendant Amy Lucier is denied. Under CCP § 473(a) a court may, "on motion of either party after notice to the other party, set aside any void judgment or order." The court finds there is no good cause to grant the motion and Defendant would suffer prejudice should the court grant the motion. rulings.Case Title: WATCHANAPORN CHANGTO AND SAKSEE ISANPAYU VS. 2001 THAI STICK, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Compliance With Discovery Order And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants 2001 Thai Stick, Inc.; Thai Stk, Inc.; Puasak Polemahasuppapole
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFFS WATCHANAPORN CHANGTO AND SAKSEE ISANPAYU'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Discovery Order And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants 2001 Thai Stick, Inc.; Thai Stk, Inc.; Puasak Polemahasuppapole. Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted. No opposition filed. Sanctions awarded as follows: (a) Issue sanctions conditionally suspended pending compliance with February 25, 2015 discovery order within five (5) days. If defendants fail to provide complete responses per February 25, 2015 discovery order within five (5) days, then issue sanctions are granted as requested. (b) Monetary sanctions granted against defendants but not counsel in the amount of $1,200. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to nrosenquest@earthlink.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: WARREN W. WHITE et al VS. PRESIDENTIAL LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY et al (Defts: Montoya and JLM Challenge Judge Quinn)
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 5, Part 1 of tentative ruling, DEFENDANTS PRESIDENTIAL LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY, ATHENE ANNUINTY & LIFE ASSURANCE COMPANY AND ATHENE ANNUITY & LIFE ASSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication The motion for summary judgment is denied. The motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. Defendant is liable for Mr. Montoya's conduct. Annuities are regulated as a form of life insurance. See Ins.C. § 101. Only licensed life agents are authorized to sell annuity products. See Ins.C. §§ 32, 1622, 1626. A "life agent" is a person authorized by and on behalf of a life, disability, or life and disability insurer to transact life, disability, or life and disability insurance. See Ins. 1622. Defendant relies on inapposite auto insurance cases to argue that Mr. Montoya was decedent's agent. Defendant fails to maintain its initial burden of production on the professional negligence, negligence, elder abuse, and rescission causes of action. Defendant and Mr. Montoya violated their statutory duties under secs. 10509.914 and 10509.915 of the Insurance Code. Defendant never trained Mr. Montoya (Montoya Depo., 42:23-43:5; 62:19-24) and Mr. Montoya did not properly explain the import of decedent's "rated age" to decedent. (Montoya depo., 52:4-24). Plaintiff's expert (Mr. Granger) explains the misunderstanding at par. 44 of his declaration. See also (Wiener Depo., 90:3-23). Defendant miscites Das v. Bank of America, N.A., (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 727, for the proposition that plaintiff cannot rely on various sections of the Insurance Code to establish negligence per se because those provisions do not provide for a private right of action. In Das, the court ruled that the plaintiff could not rely on a reporting statute because the statute contained a provision affirmatively foreclosing the use of the reporting duty for purposes of a negligence claim. In this case, the Insurance Code provisions contain no such language and the general rule of negligence per se applies. Defendants' statements were misleading and support liability on the elder abuse and rescission claims. "A person or entity shall be deemed to have taken, secreted, appropriated, obtained, or retained property for a wrongful use if, among other things, the person or entity takes, secretes, appropriates, obtains, or retains the property and the person or entity knew or should have known that this conduct is likely to be harmful to the elder ...." Id. at 736 (emphasis in original). The court grants summary adjudication on plaintiff's breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing cause of action. Plaintiff is not bringing a common law claim and cannot do so because defendant complied with the express terms of the annuity. This claim is based solely on Insurance Code sec. 785, but there is no private right of action under that provision. Abbit v. ING USA Annuity & Life Ins. Co., 999 F.Supp.2d 1189, 1198 fn.8 (S.D. Cal. 2014). The court denies summary adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages. Defendant failed to maintain its burden of production and show the absence of malicious/fraudulent conduct. Defendant argues that no officer, director, or managing agent engaged in the wrongful conduct, but defendant's separate statement does not provide any information regarding that issue.

rulings.Case Title: JAMES MONTOYA VS. UNIVERSITY OF PACIFIC, SCHOOL OF DENTRISTY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Dr. William Huggett To Comply With Subpoena Duces Tecum And Produce Psychiatric Records Of Plaintiff James Montoya; And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And Or Dr. Huggett
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 4, DEFENDANTS UNIVERSITY OF THE PACIFIC AND ARTHUR A DUGONI SCHOOL'S Motion To Compel Dr. William Huggett To Comply With Subpoena Duces Tecum And Produce Psychiatric Records Of Plaintiff James Montoya; And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And Or Dr. Huggett (Part 1 of 2 For Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling). Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court wishes the parties to address the following: (1) Notice of the motion. This is a motion to compel a business records subpoena without a personal appearance by the custodian of records. Under CCP 2025.480, notice of the motion must be given to Dr. Huggett. The notice itself is not directed to Dr. Huggett but to the plaintiff. The register of actions reflects overnight delivery of the motion to Hassard Bonnington, purportedly on behalf of Dr. Huggett, but no records prior to the filing of the motion appear to authorize service on Hassard Bonnington for Dr. Huggett. At best there is a supplemental declaration and correspondence in June, but this is post service of the motion. Consequently, the Court has a serious question whether Dr. Huggett received adequate notice of this motion, giving the court jurisdiction to order compliance with the records subpoena in any form. The lack of an appearance by Dr. Huggett in this motion cannot be construed as non-opposition to the motion. (2) The limited scope of permissible discovery. Plaintiff has a strong privacy interest in the records. His suit, involving terminated employment in December 2013, does not put all of plaintiff's subsequent medical or psychiatric history at issue. To the extent that an IIED claim has been made, the operative events occurred during the employment term, not after it. Consequently, a question arises concerning the scope and duration of any mental health records for treatment post termination. While the medical bills for mental health treatment might be sufficient to overcome the privacy objection on the issue of the amount of special damages, the treatment notes and diagnosis of any post termination mental health condition are not necessarily "necessary" to the defense of a discrimination claim based on physical disability. The medical authorization did not constitute a waiver of the privacy privilege nor did the filing of the suit. By its terms, the medical authorization was revocable at any time. In re Lifschutz does not confer a blanket right to psychiatric records but recognizes a limited waiver depending on the facts and circumstances of the case. (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY SWAN VS. EPISCOPAL COMMUNITY SERVICES
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Response To Interrogatory And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 7, PLAINTIFF KIMBERLY SWAN'S Motion To Compel Further Response To Interrogatory And For Sanctions Plaintiff Kimberly Swan's Motion to Compel Further Interrogatory Responses and for Sanctions is DENIED. The Motion seeks to compel Defendant Episcopal Community Services of San Francisco to disclose the "address and telephone numbers of Rasheedah Blake", a former employee of Defendant. It is well established that a former employee has a high expectation of privacy related to personal information in their personnel file. (County of Los Angeles v. Los Angeles County Employee Relations Commission (2013), 56 Cal.4th 905) Defendant has established all 3 essential elements necessary for the Court to deny Plaintiff's motion---(1) legally protected privacy right, (2)objective and reasonable expectation of claimant's privacy, and (3)invasion of privacy serious in nature and scope. (Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1994), 7 Cal.4th 1) The Court has balanced the former employee's privacy rights as against the Plaintiff's need for disclosure and finds that Plaintiff must look elsewhere for this information. The sanction requests from both sides are DENIED. =302/eg

rulings.Case Title: DIGITAL REALTY TRUST, INC., A MARYLAND CORPORATION ET AL VS. MICHAEL F. FOUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, July 17, 2015, Line 8, DEFENDANT MICHAEL FOUST'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Denied. Brown v. Grimes (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 265, 277 establishes that "[w]hen a party's failure to perform a contractual obligation constitutes a material breach of the contract, the other party may be discharged from its duty to perform under the contract." Further, "the question of whether a breach of an obligation is a material breach, so as to excuse performance by the other party, is a question of fact." (Id.) Cross-Defendants ("DLR") submit evidence that they considered Cross-Complainant Michael Foust's ("Foust") compliance with Section 9 of the Employment Agreement to be a material benefit received in exchange for the termination payments. (Defendants' Additional Material Fact ("AMF") 12.) This raises a triable issue of material fact as to whether the breach of Section 9 of the Employment Agreement was material. Further, DLR submits evidence that on April 7, 2014, Foust received an email from an employee of DLR containing confidential financial data on DLR assets in the Asia Pacific region. (AMF 14.) DLR also submits evidence raising a triable issue of material fact as to whether this confidential information was misappropriated in order to establish a competing venture. (AMF 36.) It is undisputed that Foust was terminated on March 17, 2014. (Foust's Undisputed Material Fact 14.) Section 7 of the Employment Agreement provides that payments were to be paid within 30 days of termination. (Declaration of Thomas Haley, Ex. C, Employment Agreement, Section 7(a).) This evidence raises a triable issue of material fact as to whether Foust breached Section 9 of the Employment Agreement before the termination payment was due. In other words, because there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Foust materially breached the agreement before his right to payment arose, a reasonable jury could find that this material breach discharged DLR's duty to perform under the contract. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg
